metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A sophisticated model scores careers using semantic matching that grasps contextual equivalence beyond simple keyword matching. Layered on top is trajectory modeling, which projects a candidate's likely path rather than just their current fit. Even when both compared figures clear the legal floor, outcomes diverge, as retail retention gaps reach eighteen percent within four months. This raises an anti-discrimination argument: legally permissible scoring can still produce materially unequal results.

artifactLines: A sophisticated model scores careers using semantic matching that grasps contextual equivalence beyond simple keyword matching.
Layered on top is trajectory modeling, which projects a candidate's likely path rather than just their current fit.
Even when both compared figures clear the legal floor, outcomes diverge, as retail retention gaps reach eighteen percent within four months.
This raises an anti-discrimination argument: legally permissible scoring can still produce materially unequal results.

beats.narration_text: The claim that semantic matching understands contextual equivalence beyond keyword matching, then layers trajectory modeling on top, is the mechanism I want to interrogate, especially since both compared figures cleared the legal floor yet retail retention gaps reached eighteen percent in four months. Can you (1) explain how contextual equivalence lets the model rate two differently-worded resumes as equal, (2) show how trajectory modeling predicts that retention gap over time, and (3) argue whether clearing the legal floor is enough given the anti-discrimination concern?
